title: Agency Workers Regulations 2010
chapters: SI 2010/93 · Made 20 January 2010 · In Force 1 October 2011 · Implements Temporary Agency Work Directive 2008/104/EC · Amended by Agency Workers (Amendment) Regulations 2019 (SI 2019/724) — Swedish derogation repealed 6 April 2020
desc: The Agency Workers Regulations 2010 give agency workers the entitlement to the same basic employment and working conditions as if they had been recruited directly by the hirer, after completing a 12-week qualifying period in the same role. Day-one rights include access to collective facilities (canteen, childcare, transport) and information about job vacancies. After 12 weeks, equal treatment applies to pay, working time, rest breaks, annual leave, and overtime rates. The Swedish derogation (pay-between-assignments contracts exempting from equal pay) was repealed from 6 April 2020. Anti-avoidance provisions prevent deliberate structuring of assignments to prevent workers completing the qualifying period. Both the temporary work agency and the hirer may be jointly or individually liable. The Regulations are critical for FM sector employers who rely heavily on agency labour — the Northerly Hill demo entity operates across multiple depots with agency worker usage.

sections.title: Rights of agency workers in relation to the hirer — equal treatment after 12 weeks
sections.summary: An agency worker is entitled to the same basic working and employment conditions as they would have received had they been recruited directly by the hirer to occupy the same role, once they have completed a qualifying period of 12 calendar weeks in the same role with the same hirer. Equal treatment covers: pay (basic pay, overtime rates, shift allowances, unsocial hours premiums, hazard pay, performance-related bonuses directly attributable to the quality or quantity of work); working time (duration, night work, rest periods, rest breaks); and annual leave. It does not cover: occupational sick pay, pension schemes, redundancy pay, maternity/paternity/adoption pay beyond the statutory minimum, bonuses not related to individual performance (loyalty, long-service), or notice periods. The comparator is a real or hypothetical directly recruited worker doing the same role.

versions.description: Original commencement. Implemented Temporary Agency Work Directive 2008/104/EC with 12-week qualifying period (the Directive permitted a qualifying period to be agreed by social partners).

Agency Workers (Amendment) Regulations 2019 (SI 2019/724) repealed the Swedish derogation (regs.10–11). From 6 April 2020, all agency workers are entitled to equal pay after 12 weeks — pay-between-assignments contracts can no longer be used to opt out of equal pay entitlements.
versions.policyRationale: The Swedish derogation was identified by the Taylor Review of Modern Working Practices (2017) as creating a two-tier workforce. Its repeal was recommended to ensure all agency workers receive equal treatment on pay.

leadingCases.name: Kocur v Angard Staffing Solutions Ltd [2019] EWCA Civ 185
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the Agency Workers Regulations do not entitle agency workers to work the same number of contractual hours as comparable permanent employees. The right to equal treatment on working time relates to the terms and conditions about working time (e.g. maximum hours, rest breaks, overtime rates) — not to the quantum of hours offered.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified that equal treatment on working time means equal terms and conditions, not equal hours

leadingCases.name: Brooknight Guarding Ltd v Matei [2018] UKEAT/0309/17
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the meaning of 'pay' under the AWR and held that it encompasses basic pay and any additional elements that are directly referable to the work performed, including shift premiums and overtime rates. Employer pension contributions and occupational sick pay are excluded.
leadingCases.significance: Defined the scope of 'pay' for AWR equal treatment purposes

refs: Temporary Agency Work Directive 2008/104/EC Art.5 (principle of equal treatment)
reg.7 AWR 2010 (circumstances in which reg.5 does not apply)
reg.12 AWR 2010 (day-one rights — access to facilities and vacancy information)
reg.17–18 AWR 2010 (right not to suffer detriment; unfair dismissal protection)

sections.title: Anti-avoidance provisions
sections.summary: The anti-avoidance provisions prevent deliberate structuring of assignments to deny agency workers the 12-week qualifying period. They apply where: the agency worker has completed two or more assignments with the same hirer or connected hirers; or with the same hirer in the same or a similar role with breaks between assignments. If a tribunal determines that the most likely explanation for the structure of assignments is to prevent the agency worker completing the qualifying period, the worker is treated as having completed it and is entitled to equal treatment (plus punitive compensation). There is no anti-avoidance issue with simply ending a single assignment before 12 weeks. The provisions target repeated patterns of short assignments with the same hirer designed to prevent the qualifying period from ever being completed.

versions.description: Original provision. Anti-avoidance designed to prevent deliberate structuring of assignments to deny workers the 12-week qualifying period.

leadingCases.name: Moran v Ideal Cleaning Services Ltd [2014] UKEAT/0274/13
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the anti-avoidance provisions and held that a pattern of rotating agency workers between assignments with breaks designed to prevent the 12-week qualifying period being completed could trigger reg.9. The tribunal must assess the most likely explanation for the structure of assignments.
leadingCases.significance: Early authority on the application of anti-avoidance provisions to deliberate assignment rotation.

sections.title: Liability of temporary work agency and hirer
sections.summary: Both the temporary work agency (TWA) and the hirer are liable for breaches of the equal treatment provisions. The TWA is liable unless it can show that it obtained, or made reasonable efforts to obtain, relevant information from the hirer about the basic working and employment conditions of comparable employees, and that having obtained such information, it ensured that the agency worker's basic working and employment conditions were no less favourable. If the TWA establishes this defence, the hirer is solely liable. This shared liability model creates a compliance incentive for both the agency and the hirer to exchange accurate information about comparable terms.
sections.text:
(1) Without prejudice to any other remedy available to the agency worker, where an agency worker suffers a detriment as a result of the failure of a temporary work agency or hirer to comply with regulation 5, the agency worker is entitled to a declaration from an employment tribunal identifying the breach of that regulation and to compensation from the temporary work agency, the hirer or both.

(2) The temporary work agency is not liable to pay compensation to the agency worker under paragraph (1) if it is able to show that—
(a) it obtained, or made reasonable efforts to obtain, the relevant information from the hirer;
(b) where it obtained such information, it acted reasonably in determining the basic working and employment conditions to which the agency worker was entitled pursuant to regulation 5.

(3) Where the temporary work agency establishes the matters referred to in paragraph (2), the liability to pay compensation shifts to the hirer.

versions.description: Original provision establishing shared liability between temporary work agency and hirer.

leadingCases.name: Coles v Ministry of Defence [2015] UKEAT/0403/14
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the shared liability model and held that where the temporary work agency obtained relevant information from the hirer and acted reasonably in determining the agency worker's conditions, liability shifts to the hirer. The agency must demonstrate it made reasonable efforts to obtain the comparator information.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the operation of the shared liability model and the agency's 'reasonable efforts' defence.

refs: reg.18 AWR 2010 (complaints to tribunal — minimum 2 weeks' pay award, no maximum)

pendingAmendments: Post-Brexit — AWR 2010 now assimilated law under REUL Act 2023
ERA 2025 — zero hours provisions interact with agency worker framework (not yet in force)
Swedish derogation repealed 6 April 2020 — fully in effect

parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The AWR 2010 implemented the Temporary Agency Work Directive 2008/104/EC after prolonged negotiation. The UK secured the right to apply a 12-week qualifying period (Art.5(4) of the Directive permitted qualifying periods agreed by social partners). The 18-month gap between making (Jan 2010) and commencement (Oct 2011) was to allow businesses to prepare. The Swedish derogation (pay-between-assignments contracts exempting from equal pay) was repealed from 6 April 2020 following the Taylor Review recommendation.

precedingDocuments.title: Temporary Agency Work Directive 2008/104/EC
precedingDocuments.significance: The governing EU Directive. Established the principle of equal treatment for agency workers. The UK negotiated the 12-week qualifying period as a permitted derogation.

precedingDocuments.title: Taylor Review of Modern Working Practices
precedingDocuments.publisher: Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy
precedingDocuments.significance: The Taylor Review recommended repealing the Swedish derogation, finding it created a 'two-tier' agency workforce. The government accepted this recommendation and repealed the derogation from April 2020.

precedingDocuments.title: Agency Workers (Amendment) Regulations 2019 (SI 2019/724)
precedingDocuments.publisher: Secretary of State for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy
precedingDocuments.significance: Repealed the Swedish derogation (regs.10-11 AWR 2010). From 6 April 2020, all agency workers are entitled to equal pay after 12 weeks. Pay-between-assignments contracts can no longer opt out of equal pay.

leadingCases.name: Kocur v Angard Staffing Solutions [2019] EWCA Civ 185
leadingCases.heldText: Equal treatment on working time means equal terms and conditions, not equal quantum of hours offered.
leadingCases.significance: Scope of equal treatment — terms not hours.

leadingCases.name: Brooknight Guarding v Matei [2018] UKEAT/0309/17
leadingCases.heldText: 'Pay' encompasses basic pay and elements directly referable to work performed including shift premiums and overtime rates. Pension contributions and occupational sick pay excluded.
leadingCases.significance: Defined scope of 'pay' for AWR equal treatment.

leadingCases.name: Moran v Ideal Cleaning [2014] UKEAT/0274/13
leadingCases.heldText: Deliberate assignment rotation to prevent qualifying period completion can trigger anti-avoidance provisions.
leadingCases.significance: Anti-avoidance application to deliberate rotation patterns.
